name: skill-trigger-regression
title: The content moved out of every-turn context. Can the agent still find it?
rationale: ADR-9 moves most of the eager layer into description-triggered skills. That
trades a certainty for a probability: content that was previously in front of
the model on every single turn is now fetched only if a description matches
the user's words. The token win is real and measured. The risk is that an
answer the agent used to have simply stops arriving, and nothing notices,
because a missing answer is silent.
So this scenario is the tripwire, and C12 makes it a merge gate: no batch of
content leaves the eager layer unless E11 still passes afterwards.
It is written BEFORE the first move and run at baseline, where everything is
still eager and it must pass trivially. That baseline is the whole point. A
post-move failure is then attributable to the move rather than to the
scenario being wrong, which is a distinction you cannot make after the fact.
WHY THE CHECKS GRADE ANSWERS AND NOT SKILL INVOCATIONS
The obvious grader — 'the sage-tiers skill was invoked' — cannot be used as
the safety net, because it is guaranteed to FAIL at baseline: the skill does
not exist yet. A check that must be rewritten at the boundary it is meant to
police is not a control. So the checks assert the ANSWER survives, which is
the property that actually matters and the one that holds on both sides of
the move. If the description does not trigger, the model does not know, the
markers vanish, and the batch is blocked. That is the safety net working.
The consulted_skill grader exists for the diagnostic half, and is added to
this scenario batch-by-batch as each skill lands — telling us not just that
the answer arrived, but that it arrived on demand rather than from a fatter
eager layer than we thought we had.
WHY THESE MARKERS AND NOT OTHERS
Each marker is chosen because ONLY Sage's own content supplies it, so a model
answering from general training cannot pass by luck:
prompt 1  The cycle slug and gate_state are readable only from the seeded
manifest in .sage/work/ — and only by an agent that knows that is
where state lives (Rule 1, State First). A generic assistant asked
'what is my current cycle state' has nothing to say.
prompt 2  `sage_memory_store` is a Sage tool name. The regex also accepts
the .sage-memory/ fallback path, because Sage's own rule says to
use it when the MCP server is absent — and it is absent in the
eval workspace. Grading only the MCP call would fail the agent for
correctly following the fallback it was told to follow.
prompt 3  A regex, not a literal. The claim under test is that Sage's tier
VOCABULARY is reachable, not that the agent picks tier 3 over tier
2 for this particular ask. Pinning the tier would grade judgment
where the scenario means to grade knowledge, and would fail for a
defensible answer.
Sage-only, for E7's reason: a bare project has no cycles, no memory system,
and no tiers, so 'bare' would score the absence of a feature as a loss.
BATCH 1 (P2-T6): memory and tiers left the eager layer, so the two
consulted_skill checks are now live. They could not have been added before
the move — the skills existed, but the answers were still being served from
eager, and a check that passes for the wrong reason is worse than no check.
These graders are reachable because Claude Code emits a `Skill` tool_use
block on auto-activation ({"skill": "sage-tiers", ...}). That was probed,
not assumed — a diagnostic that cannot fire is decoration.

# Cycle: Cache eviction policy

## State

**Current phase:** implement — 2 of 5 plan tasks done
**Next step:** Task 3 — wire the eviction hook into the request path

## Context summary

The service caches upstream responses with no eviction policy, so memory grows
until the pod is OOM-killed. The plan adds LRU eviction behind a size cap.

checks.describe: it found the active cycle (state lives in .sage/work/, and it knew that)
and reported where the cycle actually is, not just that one exists
asked to remember something, it reached for Sage's memory, not the platform's
Sage's tier vocabulary is reachable (which tier it picks is its own business)
the memory answer came from the skill, not from a fatter eager layer than we thought
and so did the tier answer
